guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology — Acute migraine and status migrainosus (current)
RACGP: Management of chronic and refractory migraine
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: the source Urgency Justification frames the visit as ruling out meningitis/subarachnoid haemorrhage, but the clinician's own red-flag screen is negative (gradual crescendo onset, afebrile, no meningism, intact neuro exam, no papilloedema). The correct telehealth answer is to recognise a severe flare of a KNOWN migraine disorder and NOT dispatch 000 for a secondary-pathology mimic that the note has already screened negative.

The genuine acuity is NOT the mimic reflex — it is a treatment-delivery gap: oral rescue (sumatriptan) was vomited, the patient cannot retain any oral fluids or medication, and there is early clinical dehydration. Telehealth cannot deliver the parenteral antiemetic/analgesia and rehydration this state needs, so same-day in-person care (T3) is correct — not routine GP (under-triage) and not 000 ambulance (over-triage).

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person after-hours home visit where the clinician administered IM prochlorperazine + IM ketorolac. Breath-Ezy cannot examine, take vitals without a device, or give IM/IV therapy — it must recognise, arrange same-day in-person parenteral treatment, and safety-net.
Age (~42) and sex (female) inferred — no age or sex stated in the source note; flagged for clinician confirmation.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
